coredata.dc:title: Boosting performance of perovskite solar cells with Graphene quantum dots decorated SnO2 electron transport layers

Zhang, Chunfu
Zhang, Hairong
Dong, Hang
Chen, Dazheng

Hao, Yue

Electron transport layer
Graphene quantum dots
SnO2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of the PSCs with a structure of ITO/SnO2:GQDs/MAPbI3Cl3-x/Spiro-MeOTAD/Ag. (b) The corresponding energy level diagram of the PSCs.

(a) Device statistics of VOC , JSC , FF, and PCE of perovskite solar cells. employing ETL incorporated with or without GQDs modification. Every box exhibits the parameter distribution of 15 devices under the same operating environment. The small square symbol (□) inside the boxes represents the mean, whereas the line across the boxes represents the median, the scattered points and curves beside the boxes represent the dispersion of all device parameters (b) The best J–V curve for PSCs with SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs ETLs. (c) The IPCE spectra for the best device based on SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs ETLs. (d) Steady output characteristics of devices based on SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs ETLs tested at maximum power point.

XPS spectra of (a) Sn 3d and (b) O 1s. XPS spectra of (c) C 1s (d) Transmission spectra of glass/ITO, glass/ITO/SnO2 and glass/ITO/SnO2:GQDs samples. (e-f) AFM images of SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs substrates. The measured RMS values are (e) 2.31 nm and (f) 2.71 nm (g) UPS spectra (h) the cut-off energy (Ecut-off) region, and (i) Fermi edge (EF,edge) region for the SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs films deposited on ITO substrate.
(a and b) Top-view SEM images of SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs films grew on ITO. (c) XRD patterns (d) and PL spectra (e) of perovskite films deposited on SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs substrates.

(a) Transient photocurrent (TPC) and (b) transient photovoltage (TPV) results of PSCs based on SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs thin films. (c) The corresponding Nyquist plots measured with and without GQDs at a forward bias of 1 V under the dark condition. (d) Mott–Schottky plots of PSCs based on SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs thin films. (e) Voc dependence upon light intensity of devices based on SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs thin films. (f) J-V curves of devices under the dark condition at bias-0.2 V to −1.2 V.
The air stability of unencapsulated devices with SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs ETL.
Statistics of SnO2 composite ETL and surface modification of only SnO2 in PSCs composed of their architectures and electronic parameters.

head.ce:title: Boosting performance of perovskite solar cells with Graphene quantum dots decorated SnO2 electron transport layers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wide Bandgap Semiconductor Technology Disciplines State Key Laboratory, Shaanxi Joint Key Laboratory of Graphene, School of Microelectronics, Xidian University, Xi’an 710071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Wide Bandgap Semiconductor Technology Disciplines State Key Laboratory
Shaanxi Joint Key Laboratory of Graphene

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Wide Bandgap Semiconductor Technology Disciplines State Key Laboratory, Shaanxi Joint Key Laboratory of Graphene, School of Microelectronics, Xidian University, Xi’an 710071, China

ce:section.ce:para: Organic-inorganic hybrid lead halide perovskites have attracted considerable research interest for its superior photovoltaic properties such as excellent carrier transport, high absorption coefficients, long electron and hole diffusion length [1–3]. Depending on these superior material properties, PSCs have achieved great success in the photovoltaics society and their PCE has advanced from 3.8% to over 24% in the past few years. In the structure of PSCs, the ETL is regarded as one of the most important parts which is used to transport and extract photogenerated electrons and simultaneously block holes [4–6]. Accordingly, to obtain high performance PSCs, appropriate ETL is the essential part. Traditionally, the TiO2 is used as the ETL because of the low cost, eco friendliness, ease of fabrication and suitable energy levels. However, the TiO2 layer suffers from low electron mobility [4], sensitivity to ultraviolet illumination [5] and high temperature-sintering process. Especially, the high-temperature process is accompanied by more time-consuming process and higher energy consumption, which is not expected in commercial products. To solve this problem, low-temperature processed PSCs attract wide attention with efforts focusing on preparing new ETLs.

In comparison with the conventional TiO2 ETL, the SnO2 is deemed one of the most competitive materials in PSCs with the excellent electrical qualities of wider bandgap, higher electron mobility and deeper conduction band [6]. And, the outstanding optical feature is another advantage for the SnO2 ETL used in the photovoltaic devices [7]. SnO2 as the ETL was firstly used in desensitized solar cells (DSSCs), but their corresponding PCE was not as satisfying as TiO2-based DSSCs [8–10]. While in the PSCs, the application of SnO2 ETL acquired great success. In 2015, a number of groups selected SnO2 as ETL in their PSCs around the same time. In Dai’s report, mesoporous SnO2 nanoparticle was used as ETL with the further treatment of TiCl4 in PSCs devices, which acquired the PCE of 10.18% [11]. Ma group combined the condense SnO2 layer with the mesoporous TiO2 layer as the ETL in the PSCs architecture with the efficiency of 7.43% [12]. From then on, the investigation of SnO2 ETL took off and achieved a lot [13–15]. Various methods to fabricate low-temperature SnO2 samples such as spin-coating deposition [16–18], chemical-bath deposition [14], and atomic layer deposition (ALD) [13] have been reported. In all these methods, spin-coating method is one of the simplest ways. Recently, the planar n-i-p structure PSCs based on the low-temperature spin-coated SnO2 films have realized a very high performance, which is in touching distance with the highest level PCE [19,20]. However, there are still existing some surface issues between the SnO2 and perovskite layers due to the interface charge recombination [21]. As many of the SnO2 ETLs are prepared with low temperature process, there are often numerous trap states existing in the SnO2 aroused by the oxygen vacancies. The nonideal interface will cause the negative effect on the device performance. Metal ions doping is one common approach to decrease the trap states in SnO2 and improve the corresponding device efficiency. And lithium (Li), yttrium (Y), and stibium (Sb) have been successfully used to perfect the SnO2 quality. And, interface engineering [14,19,22] is another active avenue to further improve the device performance. By the interface engineering, ETLs could acquire better performance such as better matching conduction band, higher carrier mobility and less charge accumulation as well as more efficient electron collection. Carbon materials (C60, graphene,) [23,24] with many magical properties have been applied into modify TiO2 or SnO2 ETL with about 10% improvement in device efficiency [25]. Especially GQDs can not only enhance the conductivity but also intermediate energy levels which acquired more attention in previous reports [23] . Their researches mainly focus on the enhancement electronic properties of SnO2 ETL [31]. However, the influence of GQDs act on the crystallization of perovskite film was not well studied. Therefore, it is quite necessary to further investigate the action mechanism of GQDs from this angle.

In this work, a combination of SnO2 and GQDs nano-colloid is adopted to form a superior ETL for high performance PSCs with the planar structure of ITO/SnO2:GQDs/MAFAPbI3Cl3-x/spiro-OMeTAD/Ag framework. GQDs act as one new class of carbon nanomaterials have gained much interest for its unique competitive characteristic compared to other semiconductor QDs [26]. In this article, we demonstrate that the introduction of GQDs between SnO2 and perovskite can lead to an apparent enhancement of electron extraction and suppression of charge recombination in PSCs. The introduce of GQDs effectively enhances the JSC and VOC . As a result, a very high steady-state PCE of ~21.1% is realized. And the device stability based on the SnO2:GQDs ETL is also greatly improved compared to the SnO2-only device for the improvement of perovskite film quality.

To inspect the function of the SnO2:GQDs ETL in regular PSCs, the device based on the architecture of ITO/SnO2:GQDs/MAFAPbI3Cl3-x/Spiro-MeOTAD/Ag was designed as shown in Fig. 1 a. The corresponding energy level diagram of the PSCs is displayed in Fig. 1b. The SEM cross-section of PSCs with the architecture is exhibited in Fig. S1. SnO2 is used here as electronic transport material with GQDs decorated on it. Although the SnO2 based PSCs have acquired well performance in previous work, there are still exist surface issues between the ETL and perovskite interface. And we try to investigate the GQDs action mechanism and further improve the devices performance. The SnO2:GQDs ETL was manufactured by spin coating on the SnO2 substrate given concentration of GQDs aqueous dispersion liquid under the condition of various speeds which were divided into 1000r, 2000r, 3000r, 4000r, 5000r, 6000r, 7000r respectively. Then the complex ETL was annealed about 20 min at 150 °C. Fig. S2 compares the characteristic parameters for the SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs based devices. The study found that the samples based on the SnO2:GQDs with the GQDs aqueous dispersion liquid speed 3000r acquired the highest PCE.

The device parameters as well as the statistical data of devices are shown in Fig. 2 a. The statistical photovoltaic parameters of PSCs are measured via reverse bias scanning (scan rate = 20 mV s−1) at the simulated irradiation of AM 1.5G (100 mW cm−2) state. Every box exhibits the parameter distribution of 15 devices under the same operating environment. The results show that the JSC and VOC increased significantly with the adding of GQDs compared with the reference without GQDs. The average JSC was increased about 8% from 22.2 mA cm−2 to 24.0 mA cm−2 through the combination of GQDs and VOC was increased 3% from 1.07 V to about 1.10 V indicating the better role of SnO2:GQDs ETL. Finally, the average PCE of PSCs is enhanced from 18.0% to above 20.7%. In terms of statistics the electrical parameters of SnO2:GQDs based samples exhibited more centralized distribution and higher average PCE, which demonstrated the good reliability of the GQDs function.

The best current density – voltage (J – V) curves of different process route prepared devices are shown in Fig. 2b. The PSCs with SnO2:GQDs ETL displayed a encouraging PCE of 21.1% with a V oc = 1.11 V, J sc = 24.4 mA/cm2, and FF = 0.78, outperforming the devices derived from SnO2 with a PCE = 18.6% (V OC = 1.07 V, J SC = 22.6 mA/cm2, and FF = 0.77, respectively). The V OC and J SC showed significant increase, indicating the better role of SnO2:GQDs ETL. As is shown in Fig. 2c the integrated current density according to IPCE curves based on SnO2:GQDs and SnO2 thin films are 24.2 and 22.1 mA/cm2. The IPCE curves integrated JSC and the J – V curves measured JSC are very close with an error no more than 3% which supported the validity of the J-V result. The enhanced IPCE value should owe to the improved film light absorption and promoted conversion ability of photon to electricity. This also suggests less recombination between the SnO2:GQDs/perovskite interface. As shown in Fig. 2d the steady-state current densities were 22.6 and 21.5 mA/cm2 in the maximum power point (MPP). The corresponding PCEs were calculated to be 20.8% and 18.3% for SnO2:GQDs and SnO2 based devices respectively, which was approach to the values by the J–V curves. The steady-state output of current density and PCE values further confirmed the performance reliability. Statistics of SnO2 composite ETL and surface modification of only SnO2 in PSCs has been displayed in Table 1 . It can be found that our device performance is one of the highest values in the surface modification of SnO2 based PSCs. It's thought the GQDs may play the role of an effective bridge with promoting the charge injection ability between the perovskite and SnO2 films. Such is helpful to finally enhance the corresponding devices efficiency [24]. And the further analysis is spread in next section.

To investigate the mechanism of GQDs acting on the SnO2 ETL, optical and electrical properties analysis of SnO2:GQDs and SnO2 thin films were made in the following. To investigate the state of Sn and O being in the SnO2 thin film, XPS was implemented. In Fig. 3 a, the spectra of Sn 3d was decomposed into two peaks basing on its different states. The Sn 3d5/2 and Sn 3d3/2 peaks appear at binding energies of 486.72 eV and 495.21 eV for the 150 °C annealed SnO2 which are accord with the reported Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2 defined structure [34–36]. The spectra of O 1s in Fig. 3b spreads different peaks at 529.6, 530.5 and 533.0 eV respectively which are corresponding to metal-oxygen bonds (SnO), OH-bonds (or oxygen vacancies) and absorbed water [37,38]. In Fig. S3 the XPS peaks of Sn and O in SnO2:GQDs are almost the same with SnO2 layer. The approach XPS corroborated our assumption that the GQDs is more likely physical adsorbed on the surface of the SnO2 sample instead of chemical reaction. This also convinced by the similar XRD curves shown in Fig. S4. The XPS core spectra in Fig. 3c confirm that there are a certain amount of C elements on the surface of SnO2:GQDs ETL in contrast to SnO2 only ETL.

As expressed in Fig. 3d the transmission spectra of glass/ITO/SnO2 and glass/ITO/SnO2:GQDs different samples were compared with the glass/ITO acted as the base line. To acquire the high output photocurrent the transparent of ETL must be high enough. With deposited SnO2 or SnO2:GQDs, the transmission of the composite layer still holds approximately 80% in the visible light wavelength of 300–800 nm indicating that the GQDs has nearly negative effect on the light transmittance. The surface morphologies of SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs could obtained from the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image. In Fig. 3e and f, compared to the SnO2 only thin films (root mean square (RMS): 2.31 nm), the SnO2:GQDs thin films exhibited slightly larger surface roughness (RMS: 2.71 nm) on the indium tin oxide (ITO) substrates. It is supposed that low concentration GQDs formed discontinuous points physical adsorbed on the surface of SnO2 ETL instead of a continuous film layer which increases the roughness. The different roughness may further influence the deposited perovskite films quality which convinced in Fig. 4 a and b. The Tauc plot for both samples is demonstrated in Fig. S5, from which the optical bandgap of SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs film can be determined. Compared to the SnO2 with a bandgap of 3.97 eV, the SnO2:GQDs exhibits a rather similar band gap of 3.95 eV. Both samples have the wide bandgap indicating the less light block, which benefits for the PSCs application. In Fig. 3g the energy band structure of SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs could be investigated from UPS. As shown in Fig. 3h the Ecut-off (cut-off binding energy) of SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs are 16.4 eV and 16.7 eV with corresponding Fermi levels (EF) calculated to be 4.82 eV and 4.52 eV, respectively, from EF = Ecut-off − 21.22 eV (emission energy from He irradiation). As expressed in Fig. 3i, the valence band maximum (EVB) was determined to be 8.52 eV and 8.13 eV for SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs, respectively, which were calculated from EVB = EF − EF,edge (Fermi edge). In Fig. 1, the band alignment is schematically presented, where the conduction band minimum (ECB) values were 4.57 eV and 4.18 eV for the SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs based samples, respectively, from ECB = EVB + Eg. A more matching ECB of SnO2:GQDs would improve the efficiency of electronic transmission between the perovskite and the SnO2 films [39,40].

Since the underlying ETL layer may act on the formation of perovskite film, it is essential to explore the perovskite film formation on SnO2 and GQDs decorated SnO2 surfaces. To obtain the information of the perovskite film morphology and corresponding crystallinity, the electron microscopy (SEM) test is very necessary. As shown in Fig. 4a and b, the perovskite films deposited on SnO2:GQDs and SnO2 films possess the similar fully covered surface morphology with the grains dense, large and uniform. Whereas, the average grain size of perovskite film grown on SnO2:GQDs is bigger compared to that on SnO2 only film as shown in Fig. S6. It is supposed that distinct surface states and surface energies caused by different ETL films affect the formation of perovskite films. As expressed in Fig. S7, the perovskite layer based on SnO2:GQDs thin films exhibited slightly larger surface roughness (RMS = 13.52 nm) compared to that on SnO2 thin films (RMS: 10.71 nm). In our opinion the larger roughness is attributed to the bigger grain size which has been proved in previous work [41,42]. Fig. 4c presents the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the perovskite films on SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs substrates. Evidently, the SnO2 only based perovskite layer exhibit the weaker absorption at the visible band which consist with the lower Jsc as displayed in Fig. 2b. The light absorption intensity of SnO2:GQDs based perovskite layer is higher which explains the Jsc enhancement of corresponding PSCs. In Fig. 4d, the XRD spectra of two perovskite thin films reveal the same position diffraction peaks assigned to 14.1° and 28.4°, corresponding to the (1 1 0) and (2 2 0) crystal planes of tetragonal phase, respectively. Nevertheless, compared to the SnO2 only based sample, the perovskite film based on SnO2:GQDs exhibit higher diffraction intensity. It is implying that GQDs have an influence on the crystallinity of perovskite films.[12] The higher intensity is bound up with a growth of crystal size observed in Fig. 4a and b as well as in favor of carrier transport and extraction. As shown in Fig. 4e the perovskite film on SnO2:GQDs substrate displays more efficient PL quenching, certificating better electron extraction capability and lower density of defects. GQDs may act as a superfast electron tunnel, leading to a reduced quenching and suppressed carrier recombination of perovskite absorber [24,43]. The carrier recombination is closely related to the trap density in the perovskite film. To characterize the trap density and further carrier recombination parameter in the perovskite layers, the space charge limited current (SCLC) technique was carried out basing the electron-only devices (ITO/SnO2 or SnO2:GQDs/Perovskite/PCBM/Ag) in Fig. S8. Defects density of the perovskite layer based on SnO2:GQDs is determined to be 2.39 × 1015 cm−3, which is lower than that of SnO2 (2.76 × 1015 cm−3) indicating the better quality of SnO2:GQDs based perovskite film. And the detailed calculation process is shown in our previous work and other reports [44,45].

Fig. 5 a shows the photocurrent decay of solution processed SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs based samples measured under short circuit state. It is apparent that the devices based on SnO2:GQDs exhibit faster decay (0.95 μs) compared to the devices based on solution processed SnO2 (1.30 μs). This indicates that the charge extraction and transport process carried on more effective in the SnO2:GQDs based devices which is instrumental in the enhancement of photocurrent. The charge-recombination lifetime of device could calculate from the photovoltage image. In Fig. 5 b, the photovoltage decay of SnO2:GQDs based device is improved from 26.5 μs to 70.3 μs compared to SnO2 based devices when measured at open-circuit state. Such indicated that there are less charge recombination and fewer trap density in the devices with the adding of GQDs. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurement further supported the above conclusion. Fig. 5c gives the Nyquist plots of the devices with the SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs ETLs at the dark condition state and forward bias of 1 V. Compared with the SnO2 only device, a bigger radius in high frequency could be observed in the SnO2:GQDs based device, indicating bigger Rrec (recombination resistance) and lower charge recombination between the boundary of SnO2:GQDs and perovskite layer. As shown in Fig. S9, the same rule can be found at the full voltage range from 0 V to 1.2 V that further convinced the above conclusion. The suppressed electron recombination can be attributed to the smaller work function of SnO2:GQDs ETL that endows an intense built-in electric field in the device [46,47]. It thus provides a higher driving force to separate the photo-induced electron-hole pairs along with a wider depletion region. Such apparently recombination suppressing of charge carriers finally promotes the VOC which also affirmed by the Mott–Schottky plots results displayed in Fig. 5d. For the cell with SnO2 ETL, the built-in voltage (Vbi) is estimated to 1.15 V from the intercept of the extrapolated 1/C2-V straight line with the V-axis, while the value rises evidently to 1.21 V for that based SnO2:GQDs ETL. This larger Vbi well consolidates the greater built-in electric field in the device based on SnO2:GQDs film. In brief, our findings suggest that the outstanding photovoltaic characteristics of devices with SnO2:GQDs ETL could give the credit to the suppressed recombination, improved electronic extraction and transport capability. The desirable characteristics inescapable dependent on the appropriate band alignment of SnO2:GQDs/perovskite heterojunction decorated by GQDs, which specifies the decreased work function of SnO2:GQDs ETL [48].

In Fig. 5e VOC is selected as a function of light intensity to further explore the recombination mechanisms of light-induced charge carriers in the state of device operation. According to the linear fitting of VOC versus logarithm of light intensity, slopes of 1.76 and 2.40 k B T/q (over here k B represents Boltzmann constant, T represents absolute temperature, and q represents elementary charge) are acquired for sample based on SnO2:GQDs and SnO2 only respectively. The slope of SnO2:GQDs based is smaller than SnO2 only which indicated that fewer trap caused recombination existing in the SnO2:GQDs based sample [49]. In another way, the dark current density could response the charge transport condition in the devices as exhibited in Fig. 5f. The leakage current of SnO2:GQDs ETLs based devices is smaller compared to the SnO2 only, indicating the charge transport are more efficient which is beneficial for the improvement of JSC and FF.

The stability of PSCs is also one important parameter to evaluate the device performance. In order to examine the device stability in the ambient air we store the devices for one month under RH ≤ 30%. In Fig. 6 , the devices based on SnO2:GQDs ETL shows better stability with 80% of its initial value when kept in the ambient air for one month. While for device with SnO2 only ETLs, the PCE value deteriorates more apparent with 65% of its initial value in the same storage environment. The better air-stability is closely bound up with the improvement of perovskite/ETL interface as well as the perovskite film quality [50].

In conclusion, with the ETL decorated with GQDs, the planar PSCs achieve a maximum steady-state PCE of >21%. By the combination of GQDs, the electron extraction and transportation are improved with the better-matched energy levels in devices. These ameliorations boost the JSC and VOC and finally convince the realization of the high-performance PSCs. In our work, the stability and efficiency of PSCs are both improved by the GQDs decorating ETLs. Such way is simple and efficient that pointing out one new direction to further improve the PSCs performance.

Chunfu Zhang conceived the idea, designed the experiment and guided the experiment. Shangzheng Pang conducted most of the device fabrication and data collection and wrote the manuscript; Chunfu Zhang and ShangZheng pang revised the manuscript; Hairong Zhang, Hang Dong, Dazheng Chen, Weidong Zhu, He Xi, Jingjing Chang, Zhenhua Lin helped the device measurement and results discussion; Jincheng Zhang helped the data analysis. Y. Hao supervised the group. All authors read and approved the manuscript.
This work was partly financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant 61334002 and 61106063.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.145099.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
